section.title: The Annunciation and the Magnificat
section.sectionRationale: The angel who visited the temple now comes to a village girl. Gabriel tells Mary she will conceive by the Holy Spirit and bear the Son of the Most High, who will reign on David's throne forever. Mary, unlike Zechariah, answers in trust: I am the Lord's servant. She hurries to Elizabeth, whose unborn child leaps for joy, and there she pours out the Magnificat, a song of a God who lifts the lowly and fills the hungry. It is one movement, from the announcement of the incarnation to the praise it draws forth.
section.thesis: God stoops to a lowly virgin to give the world its King, and the humble faith of Mary answers with a song that magnifies the God who scatters the proud, lifts up the lowly, and keeps His promise to Abraham forever.
section.frameNote: Six beats: Gabriel comes to Mary; the promise of Jesus, Son of the Most High; the Holy Spirit and Mary's yes; Mary visits Elizabeth; the Magnificat; and Mary's return home.

units.label: Gabriel comes to Mary
units.note: greetings, you who are highly favored

verses.text: In the sixth month, God sent the angel Gabriel to a town in Galilee called Nazareth,
to a virgin pledged in marriage to a man named Joseph, who was of the house of David. And the virgin’s name was Mary.
The angel appeared to her and said, “Greetings, you who are highly favored! The Lord is with you.”
Mary was greatly troubled at his words and wondered what kind of greeting this might be.

units.label: The promise of Jesus, Son of the Most High
units.note: the throne of His father David

verses.text: So the angel told her, “Do not be afraid, Mary, for you have found {{charis|favor}} with God.
Behold, you will conceive and give birth to a son, and you are to give Him the name Jesus.
He will be great and will be called the Son of the Most High. The Lord God will give Him the throne of His father David,
and He will reign over the house of Jacob forever. His kingdom will never end!”

units.label: The Holy Spirit and Mary's yes

verses.text: “How can this be,” Mary asked the angel, “since I am a virgin?”
The angel replied, “The Holy Spirit will come upon you, and the power of the Most High will overshadow you. So the Holy One to be born will be called the Son of God.
Look, even Elizabeth your relative has conceived a son in her old age, and she who was called barren is in her sixth month.
For no word from God will ever fail.”
“I am the Lord’s servant,” Mary answered. “May it happen to me according to your word.” Then the angel left her.
In those days Mary got ready and hurried to a town in the hill country of Judah,
where she entered the home of Zechariah and greeted Elizabeth.
When Elizabeth heard Mary’s greeting, the baby leaped in her womb, and Elizabeth was filled with the Holy Spirit.
In a loud voice she exclaimed, “Blessed are you among women, and blessed is the fruit of your womb!
And why am I so honored, that the mother of my Lord should come to me?
For as soon as the sound of your greeting reached my ears, the baby in my womb leaped for joy.
Blessed is she who has believed that the Lord’s word to her will be fulfilled.”
Then Mary said: “My soul {{megalyno|magnifies}} the Lord,
and my spirit rejoices in God my {{soter|Savior}}!
For He has looked with favor on the {{tapeinosis|humble state}} of His servant. From now on all generations will call me blessed.
For the Mighty One has done great things for me. Holy is His name.
His {{eleos|mercy}} extends to those who fear Him, from generation to generation.
He has performed mighty deeds with His arm; He has scattered those who are proud in the thoughts of their hearts.
He has brought down rulers from their thrones, but has exalted the humble.
He has filled the hungry with good things, but has sent the rich away empty.
He has helped His servant Israel, remembering to be merciful,
as He promised to our fathers, to Abraham and his descendants forever.”
Mary stayed with Elizabeth for about three months and then returned home.

charis.gloss: Grace, favor, undeserved kindness. Gabriel tells Mary she has found favor with God. This is not a reward for her merit but the free grace of God, who chooses the lowly and unlikely to accomplish His purpose.

megalyno.gloss: To magnify, enlarge, declare great. The Latin for this word, magnificat, gives Mary's song its name. To magnify the Lord is not to make Him bigger but to let one's whole life declare and enjoy His greatness.

soter.gloss: Savior, deliverer, rescuer. Mary rejoices in God her Savior, showing that she knows her own need of saving grace. The title runs through Luke, who loves to name Jesus as the Savior for all people.

tapeinosis.gloss: Low estate, humble condition, humiliation. God looks with favor on the low place of His servant, the very theme of the song: He lifts the lowly and casts down the proud, echoing Hannah's prayer.

eleos.gloss: Mercy, steadfast covenant love, the Greek that renders the Hebrew hesed. God's mercy reaches from generation to generation to those who fear Him, and in the incarnation He remembers to be merciful to Israel as He promised Abraham.

basic.context: Six months after Gabriel visited Zechariah, the same angel comes to Mary, a young virgin in Nazareth pledged to Joseph. He greets her as highly favored and tells her she will conceive by the Holy Spirit and bear a son, Jesus, the Son of the Most High, who will reign on David's throne forever. Mary asks how this can be, and when told it is the Spirit's work, she answers in faith: I am the Lord's servant. She hurries to her relative Elizabeth, whose unborn baby leaps for joy, and there Mary sings the Magnificat, praising the God who lifts up the lowly and keeps His promise to Israel.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the annunciation alongside the visit to Zechariah: both are angelic announcements, but Mary's trusting question and glad surrender stand in gentle contrast to the old priest's doubt. The Magnificat is soaked in the Old Testament, especially Hannah's song, so Mary sings not private feelings but the great biblical theme of God overturning the proud order of the world. The key questions are who this child is and what kind of God so exalts the lowly.
basic.originalLanguages: Mary has found charis, grace, with God. Her soul megalyno, magnifies, the Lord, from which the song takes its Latin name Magnificat. She rejoices in God her soter, her Savior, because He has looked on her tapeinosis, her low estate, and shows eleos, covenant mercy, from generation to generation.
basic.typology: Mary's song deliberately echoes Hannah's prayer, casting Mary as the true daughter of Israel's hope. The promise of an everlasting throne fulfills God's covenant with David, and the child conceived by the Spirit is the long-awaited seed of Abraham through whom all nations are blessed.
basic.christ: The child is named Jesus, the Son of the Most High and Son of God, conceived by the Holy Spirit. He is the promised King of David's line whose kingdom will never end. This is the heart of the passage: God Himself is coming into the world as a human child.
basic.god: God is the Mighty One who is also merciful, who chooses the lowly, scatters the proud, fills the hungry, and remembers His promises. He is Mary's Savior, and salvation is entirely His initiative and gift.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Samuel 2 gives Hannah's song, the pattern for the Magnificat. 2 Samuel 7 records the promise of an everlasting throne to David. Isaiah 7 foretells the virgin who bears Immanuel. And Genesis 12 records the promise to Abraham that Mary sees being kept.
basic.oneStory: The King promised to David and the seed promised to Abraham now begin to arrive, not through the mighty but through a humble girl who believes God. Mary's song announces the great reversal the gospel brings: the God who lifts the lowly is coming to save.

context.literary: The second panel of Luke's paired infancy scenes deliberately parallels and surpasses the first: a greater annunciation, a greater child, a believing rather than doubting response. The passage moves from dialogue, the annunciation, to a meeting of two mothers, to poetry, the Magnificat, the first of Luke's great songs, which sets the tone for the whole Gospel's concern with the poor and lowly.
context.historical: Nazareth was an obscure village in Galilee, and betrothal was a binding first stage of marriage. For an unmarried, pledged girl to be found pregnant carried real social danger. That God chooses such a person, in such a place, is itself part of the message. The hill country of Judah lay some distance to the south, a journey Mary undertakes at once.
context.canonical: The annunciation stands at the hinge of the Testaments, where the promises to David and Abraham begin to be fulfilled in a single child. The Magnificat has been sung by the church for two thousand years. Its themes of reversal, God exalting the humble and humbling the exalted, run straight through Luke and into the ministry of Jesus among the poor and the outcast.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To announce the miraculous conception and identity of Jesus as the Son of God and Davidic King, to hold up Mary's faith as a model of glad surrender, and through her song to unveil the character of the God who saves by lifting the lowly and keeping His covenant.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The virginal conception is a unique, unrepeatable act of God, not a pattern for others. What is held out for imitation is Mary's humble faith, I am the Lord's servant, and the posture of the Magnificat, which finds joy and security in God the Savior rather than in status, wealth, or power.

debates.question: Does highly favored mean Mary was full of grace in herself?

views.label: Recipient of God's grace
views.case: The word means one who has been graced or favored by God; Mary herself calls God her Savior, showing she received grace rather than possessing a store of merit to dispense.

views.label: A uniquely honored servant
views.case: Mary is genuinely and highly honored above other women in her calling, yet the honor rests on God's free choice and grace, not on inherent sinlessness.

debates.bearing: Reading it as God's free favor guards the gospel: Mary is blessed because God is gracious, and she rejoices in Him as her Savior too.

debates.question: Are the past tenses of the Magnificat about Mary's day or the future?

views.label: Prophetic certainty
views.case: Mary speaks of God scattering the proud and lifting the lowly as already done, because in the coming of this child the decisive reversal is as good as accomplished; faith sees the future as sure.

views.label: God's settled character
views.case: The tenses also describe how God always acts, drawing on Israel's whole history; the incarnation is the supreme instance of a pattern the faithful have long known.

debates.bearing: Either way the song teaches believers to trust that God truly overturns human pride and honors the humble, and to align their hope with His values.

study.originalLanguages: Gabriel greets Mary as one who has found charis, grace, with God, not a merit of her own but His free favor. Her response overflows in song: her soul megalyno, magnifies, the Lord, the verb that gives the Magnificat its name, and her spirit rejoices in God her soter, her Savior, a title that shows she counts herself among those who need saving. The ground of her joy is that God has looked upon her tapeinosis, her low estate, and the refrain of the song is His eleos, His covenant mercy, reaching from generation to generation. So the vocabulary itself carries Luke's gospel: grace to the lowly, a Savior for sinners, and the faithful mercy of God to Israel and the world.

typology.fulfillment: Hannah's prayer over Samuel is taken up and surpassed as Mary sings over the Savior, the God who exalts the lowly, 1 Samuel 2:1-10.
God's covenant promise to David of a son whose kingdom endures forever, fulfilled in Jesus who reigns without end, 2 Samuel 7:12-16.

typology.type: The seed of Abraham through whom nations are blessed
typology.fulfillment: The promise to Abraham, remembered in the Magnificat, kept in Christ through whom all peoples are blessed, Genesis 12:3 and Galatians 3:16.

study.christ: Here the identity of Jesus is unveiled in full: He is the Son of the Most High, the Son of God, conceived by the Holy Spirit, the promised King who will sit on David's throne and reign over an endless kingdom. Elizabeth calls Mary the mother of my Lord, confessing the child's deity before He is even born. The whole passage centers on Christ: the eternal Son taking human flesh in the womb of a humble girl, so that the God who is worshiped in the Magnificat and the child growing within Mary are one and the same Lord and Savior.
study.god: The God of the Magnificat is both mighty and merciful. He is the Mighty One who does great things, holy in His name, yet He stoops to the lowly and makes Himself the Savior of a village girl. He scatters the proud, topples rulers, and sends the rich away empty, while filling the hungry and lifting the humble. He is the covenant-keeping God who remembers His mercy to Israel and His promise to Abraham. Salvation flows entirely from His gracious initiative, and the right response, modeled by Mary, is trusting surrender and joyful praise.

crossReferences.note: Hannah's song of reversal, the model behind Mary's Magnificat, praising the God who raises the poor and humbles the proud.
God's covenant with David promising an everlasting throne, fulfilled in Jesus, the Son of the Most High.
The sign of the virgin who conceives and bears a son, Immanuel, God with us.
In the fullness of time God sent His Son, born of a woman, to redeem, the incarnation Gabriel announces.

study.oneStory: The promises to Abraham and to David converge in one child. God sends His Son into the world through a lowly virgin who answers in faith, and Mary's song announces the shape of the whole gospel: a God who saves by lifting up the humble and casting down the proud. The King whose kingdom will never end has begun to come, and every generation will call Mary blessed because in her the Savior took our flesh.

sermon.title: Mary's Song
sermon.why: Keller expounds Luke 1:39-55, Mary's Magnificat, tracing what leads her to sing and the God she sings about, the Savior who regards the lowly and overturns human pride. A rich, Christ-centered handling of the Magnificat at the heart of this movement.

quiz.q: What did Gabriel tell Mary about the child she would bear?

options.text: He would be the Son of the Most High and would reign on David's throne forever.
options.note: Verses 32 and 33 give the child's divine identity and everlasting kingship.

options.text: He would be a great prophet like Elijah.
options.note: That description fits John, not Jesus; Jesus is the Son of the Most High and King.

options.text: He would be named Jesus and bring her joy.
options.note: True as far as it goes, but it omits the heart of the promise, His divine sonship and reign.

options.text: He would be a wealthy ruler in Rome.
options.note: His throne is David's, and His kingdom is not of earthly wealth or Rome.

quiz.q: How would Mary conceive this child?

options.text: The Holy Spirit would come upon her and the power of the Most High would overshadow her.
options.note: Verse 35 gives the miraculous conception by the Holy Spirit.

options.text: God would work a miracle so that the child would be holy, the Son of God.
options.note: True and stated, but it does not name the specific means, the Holy Spirit's work.

options.text: In the ordinary way, once she and Joseph were married.
options.note: Mary was a virgin; the conception was by the Spirit, not ordinary means.

options.text: Through the priesthood in the temple.
options.note: The conception had nothing to do with the temple; it was the Spirit's miracle.

quiz.q: How did Mary respond to Gabriel's announcement?

options.text: She humbly submitted, saying, I am the Lord's servant; may it happen according to your word.
options.note: Verse 38 records her trusting surrender.

options.text: She asked how it could be, since she was a virgin.
options.note: She did ask this, but her question led to faith and surrender, unlike Zechariah's doubt.

options.text: She demanded a sign before she would believe.
options.note: That was closer to Zechariah's response; Mary trusted the word.

options.text: She refused the calling out of fear.
options.note: She did not refuse; she gladly offered herself as the Lord's servant.

quiz.q: What is the central theme of Mary's Magnificat?

options.text: God is a mighty and merciful Savior who lifts up the lowly and casts down the proud.
options.note: The song's great theme is God's saving reversal, verses 46 to 55.

options.text: God has done great things for Mary personally.
options.note: True and part of the song, but it widens to God's dealings with all the humble and with Israel.

options.text: Mary is worthy of praise for her own goodness.
options.note: Mary magnifies the Lord and calls Him her Savior; the praise is His, not hers.

options.text: God rewards the rich and powerful above all.
options.note: The song says the opposite: He sends the rich away empty and fills the hungry.

quiz.q: Whom did Mary say her spirit rejoiced in?

options.text: God her Savior.
options.note: Verse 47 names God as her Savior, showing she too needed saving grace.

options.text: The Mighty One who had done great things for her.
options.note: True and closely related, but the exact phrase is God my Savior.

options.text: The child she would bear.
options.note: Her joy centers on God the Savior, though it is bound up with the child He gives.

options.text: Herself, for being chosen.
options.note: Her rejoicing is in God, not in herself.

questions: Gabriel greeted Mary as one who had found favor with God, sheer grace rather than reward. Where do you need to rest in God's free favor rather than trying to earn His acceptance?
Mary answered, I am the Lord's servant. Is there a place where God is calling you to a costly yes, and what makes it hard to surrender as she did?
Elizabeth's baby leaped for joy at the nearness of Christ. What stirs joy in you when you draw near to Jesus, and how might you seek more of that gladness?
The Magnificat celebrates a God who lifts the lowly and humbles the proud. How does that reversal challenge the way you measure success and worth?
Mary rejoiced in God her Savior, counting herself among those in need of saving. How does knowing your own need of grace shape your worship?

translationNotes.note: Highly favored translates a word meaning one who has been shown grace; the BSB footnote notes that the greeting can be rendered Rejoice, and that some manuscripts add Blessed are you among women, words that appear again in Elizabeth's greeting in verse 42.
For no word from God will ever fail is rendered in a footnote as For nothing will be impossible with God; both capture the promise that God's spoken word carries its own power to come true.
My soul magnifies the Lord translates the verb that gives the song its traditional name, the Magnificat, from the Latin. To magnify is to declare and delight in God's greatness, not to add to it.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 1:26-56. Heading, line break, note, and poem markers were skipped when assembling verse text. No em or en dashes appear in this passage; none remain.
